name: Distillery District
details: The brick-lined streets of the Gooderham & Worts Distillery are now a pleasant car-free pedestrian district crammed with art galleries, restaurants, brewpubs, coffee shops, and other independent businesses.

name: St. Lawrence Market South
details: The St. Lawrence Market south building has stood on this corner since 1842, when the central portion served as City Hall. 120 food vendors across two floors include butchers, fishmongers, organic vegetable stalls, take-out restaurants, and speciality shops. The south market is open every day of the week except Mondays.

name: Gooderham Flatiron Building
details: Often described as the most-photographed building in Toronto, the Flatiron has stood in the heart of Old Toronto since 1892. President George Gooderham (1830–1905) of the Gooderham & Worts Distillery once kept a watchful eye on his sprawling business empire from an  office in the top floor cupola.

name: Berczy Park
details: Berczy Park became a photogenic favourite when it was refurbished in 2017 and given a Claude Cormier-designed fountain decorated with 27 cast iron dogs, and one cat. Don't miss the distinctive trompe-l'oeil mural on the rear wall of the Gooderham Flatiron Building to the east, created by Derek Michael Besant in 1980.

name: Hockey Hall of Fame
details: Canada's national hockey museum features carefully curated exhibits and memorabilia related to Canada's game. Olympians, NHLers, PWHLers and junior players are all honoured here. And if you'd like a photo with the Stanley Cup, that's here, too.

name: Union Station
details: Toronto's transit hub since 1927. VIA Rail national trains, GO Transit commuter lines, UP Express airport trains, and the TTC subway all converge here. The Great Hall on the main level just off Front Street has been freshly renovated, with a soaring ceiling and Beaux-Arts finishes. Tucked away on the lower levels is a delicious mix of speciality shops, international food courts, and restaurants.

name: Maple Leaf Square
details: This normally bland space where Union Station meets Scotiabank Arena becomes the official outdoor gathering spot for hardy fans of the Toronto Maple Leafs and Toronto Raptors during playoffs runs. When the Raptors are playing, the square is rebranded as Jurrasic Park.

name: Scotiabank Arena
details: This is the shared home of the Toronto Maple Leafs NHL team and the Toronto Raptors NBA team, as well as a popular venue for major concerts and events.

name: Roundhouse Park
details: The John Street Roundhouse was built in 1929 by the Canadian Pacific Railway. Converted into a park in 1997, its massive turntable still functions. The adjoining buildings, originally used for locomotive repair, now house the Toronto Railway Museum, a brewpub, and a restaurant arcade.

name: Ripley's Aquarium
details: Canada's largest indoor aquarium opened in 2013 and quickly became a central attraction for tourists and locals alike. Highlights include photogenic neon jellyfish and a 96-metre-long underwater tunnel.

name: Rogers Centre
details: Home of the Toronto Blue Jays, Canada's only major league baseball team. When the building opened in 1989 as SkyDome, its retractable roof was revolutionary, keeping fans and players comfortable from April to October.

name: CN Tower
details: Toronto's most recognizable symbol, the CN Tower was the world's tallest free-standing structure from completion in 1976 through to 1997. On a clear day, it's possible to glimpse Niagara Falls and New York State from the observation deck.
